title: Why I Hand-Rolled a 3D Icosahedron Instead of Shipping Three.js
summary: My portfolio has a spinning 3D icosahedron on the hero. Everyone assumes it's Three.js. It's about a hundred lines of vanilla canvas math, and I want to explain why that was the right call.
content:
There's a spinning icosahedron on my portfolio's hero section, and every developer who's asked me about it has assumed the same thing: Three.js, right?

No. It's a hundred-ish lines of vanilla JavaScript drawing onto a 2D canvas.

I want to explain why, because the reasoning generalises far beyond one decorative shape.

When I started building the hero, I did what everyone does. I typed npm install three. Then I looked at what that actually meant. Three.js is around 600KB minified. Tree-shaken and gzipped you can get the core down to maybe 150KB, but you're still shipping a WebGL renderer, a scene graph, a material system, texture loaders, an animation mixer — an entire 3D engine.

And what did I need? Twelve vertices, thirty edges, and a rotation.

That's the whole requirement. An icosahedron is one of the friendliest shapes in 3D graphics. Its twelve vertices are just the cyclic permutations of (0, ±1, ±φ), where φ is the golden ratio. You can write them out by hand.

Rotating them is two matrix operations you learned in secondary school trigonometry. Projecting them onto the screen is a division by depth. Drawing them is ctx.lineTo. The core of the render loop looks like this:

const scale = fov / (fov + z);
const x2d = x * scale + cx;
const y2d = y * scale + cy;

That's perspective projection. That's the scary part. It's three lines.

Here's the thing I keep coming back to from fifteen years of building systems: every dependency is a loan. You get the functionality now, and you pay interest forever — in bundle size, in upgrade churn, in security surface, in the moment two years from now when a breaking change in a library you barely use eats an afternoon.

Sometimes the loan is absolutely worth taking. If I were building a product configurator with lighting, materials, and imported models, I would reach for Three.js without a second thought. Rewriting a physically-based renderer by hand is not craftsmanship, it's ego.

But a wireframe polyhedron is not that. The gap between what Three.js offers and what I needed was enormous, and that gap is where the interest payments live.

There were quieter benefits too. The hand-rolled version renders on a plain 2D canvas, so there's no WebGL context to lose on low-memory mobile devices — a real concern given how much of my traffic comes from phones in Southeast Asia. It costs almost nothing on the main thread. And when I wanted the edges to fade with depth and pick up my site's day-rotating accent colour, it was a five-minute change to code I fully understood, not a spelunking trip through a material system.

The deeper point is about defaults. The modern frontend reflex is: need X, install X. The reflex is fine most of the time. But it's worth pausing to ask what fraction of the library you're actually going to use. If the answer is two percent, the underlying math is often smaller and simpler than the API you'd learn to avoid it.

I'm not telling you to hand-roll your 3D engine. I'm telling you that a spinning shape on a landing page isn't a 3D engine problem — and knowing the difference is the actual skill.

Twelve vertices, thirty edges, zero dependencies. It's been running for months and I've never had to update it.

That's my favourite kind of code.

title: The Day ICP Changed How I Think About the Internet
summary: I was skeptical. Then I actually built on Internet Computer — and I haven't been able to think about web infrastructure the same way since.
content:
I'll be honest with you.

When I first heard the pitch for Internet Computer Protocol — "a blockchain that runs at web speed, hosts entire applications on-chain, eliminates cloud providers" — my reaction was the same as most developers: yeah, sure it does.

I'd heard too many grand claims by then. Three years of building on EVM chains had given me a healthy cynicism about anything that promised to "fix the internet."

So I ignored it for about six months.

Then Dfinity opened their Developer Grant Programme, and a colleague sent me the link. Not because he was excited about ICP. Because the grant money was real and the application deadline was in two weeks.

That's genuinely why I applied.

What happened next I didn't expect.

I started actually reading the technical whitepaper. Not the marketing landing page — the actual paper, with the threshold BLS signatures and the chain-key cryptography and the subnet architecture. And somewhere around page forty I stopped skimming and started taking notes.

This thing is weird in the best possible way.

On Ethereum, your smart contract lives on-chain. Your frontend lives on AWS. Your database lives on Google Cloud. You write "decentralized application" in your pitch deck but three of your four dependencies are Jeff Bezos's servers. The chain is decentralized. Everything around it isn't.

ICP flips this. Canisters — the ICP equivalent of smart contracts — can serve HTTP requests directly. Your frontend HTML, your backend logic, your state, all running inside the consensus layer. No cloud. No CDN. No AWS bill arriving on the first of the month.

I built my first canister in Motoko on a Tuesday afternoon. By Thursday I had a working product tracking system serving real web pages straight from the blockchain.

I sat there staring at the browser and thought: this is actually insane.

Not insane in the hype sense. Insane in the quiet way where you realise something fundamentally changed and you didn't notice until it was already done.

The Dfinity grant came through in November 2022. Grant ID 22531594, if you want to look it up. By that point I'd already rebuilt our entire product tracking infrastructure on ICP. Not because we had to. Because once you see what's possible, it's hard to go back to stitching together cloud services and calling it "decentralised."

There's a version of Web3 that's just Ethereum with a MetaMask button. And there's a version that actually rethinks where computation lives.

I know which version I find more interesting.

If you're an EVM developer who's never touched ICP, I'm not saying abandon what you know. I'm saying: spend one weekend on it. Read the actual documentation, not the Twitter threads. Build something small.

You might end up taking notes too.

title: Motoko vs Solidity: What Nobody Tells You Before You Switch
summary: After years writing Solidity and then switching to Motoko for ICP, here's what the comparison articles always leave out.
content:
Every "Motoko vs Solidity" article I've read was written by someone who read the documentation.

I've written production code in both. The comparison looks very different from that angle.

Let me tell you the things the tutorials skip.

Solidity's gas model is simultaneously its greatest discipline and its most exhausting constraint. Every line of code you write, you're mentally running a gas meter. Loop over an array? How big could that array get? Emit an event? What's the calldata cost? After a while it becomes second nature — but it never stops being work. You write leaner code, yes. You also spend twenty minutes deciding whether to use uint256 or uint128 in a struct to save a few thousand gas.

Motoko doesn't have this. Cycles exist on ICP, but they're not the same cognitive overhead. You're not gas-optimizing every function call. You're just... writing software. Which sounds obvious until you've spent three years not doing that.

The thing that surprised me more was the actor model.

Motoko canisters are actors. They process messages asynchronously, maintain their own state, communicate via message passing. If you've written Erlang or Elixir, this clicks immediately. If you haven't — and most blockchain developers haven't — there's a learning curve that nobody warns you about.

The gotcha is async/await. In Solidity, your function runs synchronously, atomically. It either succeeds completely or reverts. In Motoko, an await can pause your function mid-execution, and state can change in that gap. I got burned by this exactly once on a DeFi prototype. The kind of burn you only need once.

Orthogonal persistence is the other thing.

In Solidity, your contract state persists because it's on-chain. You think about it constantly — every SSTORE is expensive, every state variable is a decision. In Motoko, your canister's state just... persists. Through upgrades. Through subnet restarts. You declare a stable variable and it survives everything. It feels almost too easy. And then you realise how much mental overhead you were carrying before and never noticed.

The downside? Tooling. Solidity has Hardhat, Foundry, a decade of community libraries, every possible audit tool. Motoko has a solid standard library and a community that's growing but isn't there yet.

Neither language is better. They solve genuinely different problems.

Solidity is the right tool for EVM ecosystems, DeFi protocols that need composability across chains, anything where you need the existing Ethereum developer surface. Motoko is the right tool when you want your entire application on-chain, when you want the actor model's concurrency benefits, when you're building something that needs to serve the open web directly from the protocol layer.

I use both. Not because I can't choose. Because the use cases are actually different.

What I wish someone had told me: budget two weeks before you feel productive in Motoko if you're coming from Solidity. Not because it's harder — it isn't. Because it's different in ways that require you to unlearn things before you can learn new things.

That two weeks is worth it.

title: What Applying for a Dfinity Developer Grant Actually Looks Like
summary: I went through the Dfinity Developer Grant Programme in 2022. Here's the honest version of what that process was — not the press release version.
content:
The official description of the Dfinity Developer Grant is clean and encouraging. "Supporting developers building on Internet Computer." Great. Helpful. Tells you nothing about what actually happens.

Here's what actually happens.

You write a proposal. Not a pitch deck — a proper technical proposal. They want to know what you're building, why ICP specifically, what milestones you'll hit, and how you'll spend the money. The "how you'll spend the money" section is where most developers get vague and where the reviewers notice.

I spent more time on the budget breakdown than on the technical architecture. That felt backwards until I realised: a grant committee sees a hundred "we're going to build a revolutionary DApp" proposals. They don't see many proposals where someone has clearly thought through three months of actual development costs to the line item.

The technical evaluation is real. They're not just checking whether you can write smart contracts. They're checking whether your architecture makes sense on ICP specifically, whether you've understood the canister model, whether you're actually using what ICP offers or just porting an EVM project and calling it an ICP project.

The milestone structure is the part I appreciated most. Rather than paying out a lump sum and hoping for the best, Dfinity ties disbursements to deliverables. You hit Milestone 1, you get Tranche 1. This forced a discipline I'm genuinely grateful for.

The product we delivered in November 2022 was a blockchain product tracking system. Not a whitepaper. Not a prototype demo. A system in production, running on ICP, serving real supply chain data through canisters.

Would I apply for the grant again? Without hesitation.

Not just for the funding. For the structure it imposed on the project. And honestly, for the forcing function of having to explain your technical decisions to reviewers who know what they're looking at.

If you're building on ICP and you have a real project, apply. Do the budget breakdown properly. Be specific about milestones. Show them you've actually written Motoko.

That last part matters more than you think.

title: I Got My Smart Contract Audited. Here's What They Found.
summary: Getting a third-party audit on your Solidity contracts is humbling. It's also one of the most useful things you can do.
content:
Getting your smart contracts audited for the first time is a uniquely humbling experience.

You've been staring at the same code for months. You've run every test. You've had colleagues review it. You are quite confident this thing is solid.

Then the audit report comes back.

The first section is the critical findings. You read the first one and your stomach does something unpleasant.

Let me tell you what they found in ours.

The one that embarrassed me most was a reentrancy vulnerability I'd introduced by refactoring. The original code was fine. Then I "cleaned it up" — moved some logic around, made the function look neater — and in doing so I changed the order of a state update and an external call. Checks-Effects-Interactions is the pattern every Solidity developer knows from week one. I violated it during a refactor.

The second finding was an integer overflow in a calculation function. A pre-SafeMath section of the codebase that hadn't been updated when we moved to Solidity 0.8.x. The auditors caught it. My test suite hadn't covered that specific edge case.

The medium-severity findings were more interesting because they were less obvious. Access control issues where a restriction could be bypassed through a specific call sequence. Gas griefing vectors. An event emitted before state was finalised.

None of these were things I was stupid for missing. They require adversarial thinking that's genuinely hard to maintain on your own code.

What changed after the audit: I now think about CEI on every single state change as a reflex. I added invariant testing and fuzz testing to our suite. I stopped cleaning up code that works — ugly code that's correct is better than elegant code with a reentrancy vulnerability.

The audit cost money. It took three weeks. It caught things that would have cost orders of magnitude more if exploited.

Get your contracts audited. Go in expecting to be humbled. Come out writing better code.

title: Building DeFi on BNB Smart Chain: The Practical Reality
summary: BNB Smart Chain gets a lot of criticism in developer circles. Having built DeFi protocols on it, I have more complicated feelings.
content:
The developer community has a complicated relationship with BNB Smart Chain.

On crypto Twitter, BSC is the chain you apologize for. "Yeah, it's fast and cheap, but it's centralized" is the standard disclaimer you attach to any positive statement about it, like a warning label.

I've built DeFi protocols on it. The reality is more interesting than the discourse.

Let's start with what's true about the criticism. BSC's consensus mechanism — Proof of Staked Authority — involves a small validator set. Twenty-one active validators at any given time, all approved by Binance. That's not the decentralization story Ethereum tells.

Now let's talk about what the criticism misses.

Transaction fees on Ethereum during DeFi summer 2021 were genuinely hostile to users. Forty, fifty, eighty dollars for a swap. At those prices, DeFi stops being accessible to anyone who isn't moving large amounts. BSC fees in the same period were cents. For users in Southeast Asia — where I'm building, where average incomes are not San Francisco incomes — that difference is not cosmetic. It's the difference between being able to use the product or not.

The EVM compatibility also matters more than people who've only deployed on Ethereum tend to acknowledge. Every Solidity contract you've written deploys unchanged. For a small team building a real product under real time pressure, that's significant.

Here's my honest take: BSC is not the right answer to "where should DeFi exist long term." The centralization trade-offs are real.

But it's a very practical answer to "where can I deploy a DeFi product today that ordinary users in Southeast Asia can actually afford to use."

Those are different questions. Both are worth asking.

The developers who look down on BSC from their Ethereum maximalism are often the same developers whose products are only usable by people with thousands of dollars in their wallets.

I'm more interested in who can use the thing I build than where it lives.

title: NFTs Beyond the JPEG: What the Hype Missed
summary: The NFT boom and crash focused everyone on speculative art. That was always the least interesting thing about the technology.
content:
When the NFT market peaked in early 2022, I was getting asked the same question at every dinner party: "Do you think this will keep going up?"

I kept saying the same thing: I don't know, and honestly I'm not that interested.

People thought I was being falsely modest. I was being completely sincere.

Here's what I actually find interesting about NFTs: not the speculative art market. The infrastructure underneath it.

A non-fungible token, at its technical core, is a cryptographic proof of provenance for a unique asset. Who created it. When. Who has owned it since. What rights are attached to it. All of this encoded in a smart contract, immutable, publicly verifiable.

That's a genuinely powerful primitive. The JPEG is just one application of it. And not even the most interesting one.

Supply chain provenance is more interesting. I built a blockchain product tracking system at Iota Technologies. Each unit of product gets a token. Every transfer of custody is recorded on-chain. Any party in the chain can verify the complete history without trusting any single intermediary.

The counterfeit goods problem in Asia is enormous. Billions of dollars. Affecting everything from pharmaceuticals to luxury goods to industrial components where fakes can get people killed. A properly designed on-chain provenance system doesn't have the single point of failure that centralised verification has.

Digital credentials are another one. Your university degree is a piece of paper or a PDF. Both can be forged. An institution that issues academic credentials as NFTs creates a verification path that doesn't require contacting the issuing institution — the verification is in the chain itself.

Real estate title. Event ticketing without scalping. Digital media licensing where creators receive royalties on secondary sales.

None of these require expensive JPEGs or speculative asset dynamics. They require a blockchain, a smart contract, and a clear model of what provenance means for that asset class.

The hype cycle embarrassed the technology. The underlying primitive is still there, still useful, still largely unexplored for the applications that matter most.

The dinner party question was always the wrong question.

title: Why We Migrated Part of Our Stack to Polygon
summary: A practical account of moving a specific workload from Ethereum to Polygon — what worked, what didn't, what we learned.
content:
I want to tell you about a specific decision, not a general opinion.

In early 2023, we had a set of microtransaction-heavy operations running on Ethereum mainnet. High-frequency state updates, small value transfers — the kind of on-chain activity that makes complete sense conceptually and becomes eye-wateringly expensive when gas fees are elevated.

The gas costs were eating the economics of the product. Not slowly — substantially. We had to do something.

We evaluated Arbitrum, Optimism, and Polygon PoS. I'm not going to tell you which one is "best" in general because that's not a meaningful answer. I'll tell you why we chose Polygon for this specific workload.

The first factor was finality time. For user-facing microtransactions, optimistic rollup challenge periods create UX uncertainty. Polygon PoS gave us sub-two-second finality.

The second factor was ecosystem maturity. The Graph, IPFS, Chainlink — all had mature Polygon support. We didn't have to rebuild our data infrastructure.

The third factor was the bridge. Moving assets between Ethereum and Polygon is well-documented and predictable.

What didn't work: bridge latency for the Ethereum → Polygon direction is meaningful for assets that need to move frequently in both directions. We ended up keeping higher-value, lower-frequency operations on mainnet.

The migration itself took about three weeks of engineering time. EVM compatibility meant our contracts deployed unchanged.

The outcome: gas costs for that workload dropped by roughly 98%. The product economics that hadn't worked on mainnet started working.

That's the whole story. Not a philosophy, just a decision with specific context and specific results.

title: Why I Still Reach for Node.js When Building Blockchain Backends
summary: In a world of Golang microservices and Rust performance worship, I want to make the case for Node.js as a blockchain backend choice.
content:
I'm going to say something that will annoy some people.

For most blockchain backend services — the kind that index events, serve API data, handle webhooks from on-chain activity, manage queues of transactions to submit — Node.js is a completely sensible choice and the performance arguments against it are mostly theoretical.

The first reason is ecosystem alignment. Ethers.js and Web3.js are JavaScript-native. The entire blockchain development ecosystem is written for Node.js first. When you're working with a Hardhat project, building off-chain services in Node.js means you're in the same language as your tests, your scripts, your deployment tooling.

The second reason is that most blockchain backends are I/O-bound, not CPU-bound. What does a blockchain indexing service actually spend its time doing? Waiting for RPC responses. Waiting for database writes. The event loop model is extremely well-suited to this workload.

The Golang argument — "Go is faster" — is often technically correct and practically irrelevant. If your service is spending 80% of its time waiting on Infura responses, optimizing your local computation speed moves a very small needle.

Where I do reach for Go: services that need genuine CPU-intensive work, cryptographic operations in hot paths, services that need extremely predictable latency at high concurrency.

The thing I've found after years of building blockchain infrastructure: the performance bottleneck is almost never in your application server. It's in your RPC provider. It's in your database query patterns. It's in your caching strategy.

Fix those first. Then worry about whether Node.js or Go is marginally faster.

TypeScript on Node.js gives you type safety, strong tooling, excellent async primitives, and the largest backend ecosystem in existence. For a team that's also writing Solidity and Motoko and frontend JavaScript, keeping the backend in the same language family reduces cognitive overhead.

That's not the exciting answer. It's the one that's worked for me.

title: The TRON Ecosystem: An Honest Assessment
summary: TRON has a reputation problem with Western developers. The technical reality is more nuanced than the memes suggest.
content:
TRON has a reputation in Western developer circles that I don't think is entirely deserved.

The memes write themselves: Justin Sun drama, centralization concerns, the stablecoin dominance being "not real DeFi." I understand the criticisms. Some of them are fair.

But I've deployed on TRON, and the technical experience is not what the discourse suggests.

The first thing you notice: TRON processes a staggering amount of actual economic activity. USDT on TRON handles more daily transaction volume than most chains people treat as serious. Not speculative volume — real transfers of real stablecoin value between real users and businesses, particularly in East and Southeast Asia.

That's not nothing. User adoption at scale tells you something that developer opinions don't.

The EVM compatibility story on TRON is imperfect. TVM is compatible with Solidity but with differences that bite you if you're not careful. The resource model — Energy and Bandwidth instead of gas — requires learning. The tooling is less mature than Ethereum's.

Where TRON makes sense in practice: if your target users are in Asia and already have TRX in their wallets, meeting them where they are is worth something. High-frequency USDT transfers are genuinely cheap and fast on TRON.

What I'd tell a developer evaluating TRON: don't take the Western developer discourse as the whole picture. Go look at the actual usage data. Go understand who is actually using this chain and for what. Then decide whether that user population overlaps with the users you're trying to serve.

Technical elegance is worth pursuing. It's not the only thing worth pursuing.

title: Vue.js for DApp Frontends: What Works, What Doesn't
summary: The React ecosystem dominates DApp frontend development. Having built DApp UIs in Vue.js, I think the alternative is underrated.
content:
Every DApp frontend tutorial defaults to React. Ethers.js + React + wagmi. That's the standard stack, and it works.

I've built production DApp frontends in Vue.js. The choice wasn't ideological — it was pragmatic — but I've come to genuinely prefer Vue for this class of application.

Here's what surprised me.

Vue's reactivity system maps very naturally to blockchain state management. On-chain state changes — balances updating after a transaction confirms, allowances changing, contract events arriving — these are fundamentally reactive data problems. Vue's computed properties and reactive refs make this feel natural.

The composables pattern in Vue 3 is where things get interesting for Web3 specifically. I built a set of composables — useWallet, useContract, useTransaction — that handle the common patterns: connecting a wallet, loading a contract instance, sending a transaction and tracking its lifecycle. The component doesn't know anything about ethers.js internals. It just binds to reactive state.

The ecosystem gap is real and I won't pretend otherwise. wagmi has a rich React ecosystem with no Vue equivalent. RainbowKit, ConnectKit — most wallet UI libraries are React-only. You're building more from scratch in Vue.

For a team that already knows Vue, I think the tradeoff is favorable — you gain productivity in a familiar ecosystem at the cost of some Web3-specific library availability.

But if you've been told Vue isn't suitable for DApps, that's not right. It's suitable. It's just not the default choice.

Sometimes the non-default choice fits your situation better.

title: How Blockchain Actually Solves the Counterfeiting Problem
summary: We built a blockchain product tracking system. Here's the specific technical problem it solves and why traditional database approaches fail at it.
content:
Let me describe a specific problem before I talk about how blockchain solves it.

A manufacturer produces ten thousand units of a product. Each unit moves through a supply chain: manufacturer to regional distributor to local distributor to retailer to consumer. At each step, documentation is created. A certificate of authenticity. A shipping manifest.

Now I want to ask: is the unit I'm holding in my hand the genuine article?

The traditional answer is: check the documentation. Scan a QR code that queries a central database. Here's the problem: the documentation can be forged. The database is controlled by a single party. If the database is compromised, or the administrator is corrupt, the entire verification chain fails.

Pharmaceutical counterfeiting kills people. Industrial component counterfeiting causes equipment failures. This is not a hypothetical problem.

Blockchain changes the trust model.

In our product tracking system, each product unit is registered on-chain at manufacture. The registration is a transaction signed by the manufacturer's private key — unforgeable without that key. Every subsequent transfer of custody is also an on-chain transaction. To verify a product, you look at the on-chain history. No single party controls that history.

The immutability matters as much as the distribution. Once a transaction is recorded on-chain, it can't be altered. The audit trail doesn't have a delete button.

We built this on ICP for a specific reason: the canisters can serve verification requests directly. A consumer scans a QR code, their browser makes a request to a canister, the canister returns the on-chain provenance. No intermediary server that could be spoofed or hacked.

The limitation to be honest about: this solves the problem of verifying whether a product's recorded history is authentic. It doesn't solve whether the initial registration was accurate. Blockchain makes the downstream chain of custody trustworthy. The initial registration still requires trusted parties.

But "trustworthy chain of custody after genuine registration" is already a massive improvement over "trust a central database that can be compromised at any point."

That gap is where counterfeiting lives. Closing it is worth building for.

title: From System Administrator to Blockchain Developer: What Actually Transfers
summary: I started as a junior sysadmin. I ended up leading blockchain development. The path was less of a pivot than you might think.
content:
People ask me sometimes how I ended up in blockchain after starting in system administration. The implicit assumption is that there's a dramatic career pivot in the story.

There isn't really.

When I was at Bayer Southeast Asia, managing servers and backup systems and monitoring infrastructure, I was spending a lot of time thinking about: How do you ensure data integrity? How do you make systems that recover from failure? How do you make sure that a backup actually contains what you think it contains?

These are, at a different level of abstraction, exactly the problems that blockchain infrastructure deals with.

Distributed systems thinking transfers completely. Running a blockchain node, understanding how a network of nodes reaches consensus, thinking about what happens when a node fails — none of this is alien if you've managed server infrastructure.

Understanding networking and protocols transfers. Thinking about latency, about packet loss, about the difference between synchronous and asynchronous communication.

The security mindset transfers. A sysadmin who's good at the job is always asking: who has access to what? What's the blast radius if this fails? These are exactly the questions you ask when designing smart contract systems.

What didn't transfer directly: the cryptography (had to learn this deliberately), the economics (DeFi protocol design involves game theory I've learned on the job), and the programming model (smart contract development required new ways of thinking about computation).

The honest summary: if you're in infrastructure or systems work and you're curious about blockchain, your background is more relevant than you think. The cryptography and economics can be learned. The distributed systems intuition takes years to develop, and you might already have it.

The path I took wasn't a leap. It was a climb. Most of the holds were already familiar.

title: The Web3 UX Problem Nobody Wants to Admit
summary: Blockchain technology is genuinely powerful. The user experience of interacting with it is, in most cases, genuinely terrible. These two things can both be true.
content:
I'm going to say the quiet part loud.

Most Web3 applications have terrible user experience. Not "could be improved" terrible — "actively drives away the users it needs to work" terrible.

I say this as someone who builds these applications.

The seed phrase problem is the first and most obvious one. Twelve words that, if lost, mean you've permanently lost access to your assets. No recovery. No customer support. No reset password email. This is presented as a feature — "you are your own bank" — and it is genuinely a feature for a small number of sophisticated users. For everyone else, it's a terrifying responsibility that most normal people correctly decide they don't want.

The gas fee complexity compounds it. Before you can do the thing, please estimate how much you're willing to pay in a unit (Gwei) that doesn't map to anything you already understand. And if you set it too low, the thing fails and you've still paid.

I've watched non-technical users try to use DApps for the first time. They hit the wallet connection step and they're already confused.

Here's what frustrates me most: the technical community's response is often "the users need to be educated." This is completely backwards. It's the builders' responsibility to meet users where they are.

The good news is that this is solvable. Account abstraction — ERC-4337 — enables smart contract wallets with social recovery and gas sponsoring. ICP's Internet Identity offers passkey-based authentication that removes seed phrases entirely.

The technology exists to build DApps as easy to use as Web2 applications. The question is whether builders will prioritize this.

Every time I'm about to ask a user to sign a transaction, I ask: does this need to be a transaction? Can I abstract this away?

The applications that win the next wave of Web3 adoption will be the ones you can give to your parents.

We're not there yet. We need to be.

title: PHP Is Not Dead: Enterprise Web Development in 2025
summary: The 'PHP is dead' narrative is as wrong in 2025 as it's been for the last fifteen years. Here's the case for PHP in enterprise contexts.
content:
Every two years or so, a wave of articles announces that PHP is finally, definitively dead.

PHP is not dead. PHP runs WordPress, which powers about 40% of the internet. PHP runs Laravel, which is one of the most thoughtfully designed web frameworks I've used in any language.

Laravel specifically deserves a real conversation rather than the dismissal it gets from people who last used PHP in 2008.

The Eloquent ORM is genuinely elegant. The relationship definitions, the query scopes, the eager loading — well-designed APIs that a new team member can understand in a day. I've onboarded junior developers onto Laravel projects and watched them become productive in a week.

The Artisan console is one of the better developer experience features in any framework. Code generation, migration management, queue work, scheduled tasks — all through a consistent CLI interface.

On performance: yes, Node.js and Go and Rust are faster in raw benchmarks. For most enterprise web applications, the bottleneck is not application server throughput. It's database performance, query optimization, caching strategy. I've run PHP applications that handle millions of page views per day without notable performance issues.

The context where I would not choose PHP: compute-intensive services, real-time systems, anything where you need sub-millisecond response times.

But a business administration system? An internal ERP? A content management API? A backend for a DApp that needs administrative functions?

Laravel is my default choice. It's boring in the right way — reliable, well-understood, easy to hand off. After fifteen years I still enjoy writing it.

Dead languages don't get that reaction.

title: Integrating Blockchain into an Existing ERP System
summary: Nobody talks about the messy work of connecting blockchain to legacy enterprise systems. Here's what it actually involves.
content:
The blockchain integration stories you read about are almost always greenfield: a new system, built from scratch, designed around blockchain from the ground up.

Real enterprise technology work is rarely greenfield.

You have an existing ERP system. It works. It has five years of data in it. Management wants to "add blockchain." You have to figure out what that means without breaking the system that's already running the business.

The first decision is what, specifically, goes on-chain. Not everything should. On-chain storage is expensive and slow compared to a traditional database. The question is: what data needs the specific properties that blockchain provides — immutability, decentralisation, auditability without trusting a single party?

In an ERP context, the answer is usually a subset of transaction records. The audit trail for specific high-stakes operations: asset transfers, compliance-relevant transactions, multi-party agreements.

The integration architecture: we used event-driven for high-priority transactions and batch for historical audit records. The ERP emits events through its existing webhook system. A Node.js service receives these, validates them, and submits the relevant data to the smart contract. The transaction hash gets written back to the ERP record as proof of on-chain registration.

The latency problem: blockchain transaction finality is slower than database writes. You cannot make the ERP user wait for on-chain confirmation. The solution is to treat blockchain recording as an asynchronous process. The ERP writes locally and confirms to the user immediately. The on-chain registration happens in the background.

This pattern sounds simple and the implementation is full of edge cases. Retry logic, dead letter queues, reconciliation processes, alerting for stuck transactions. Nobody puts this in their blockchain integration case study.

The honest assessment: the integration overhead is substantial. The result — immutable, independently verifiable audit records — is genuinely valuable for the right use cases.

Know which answer you're dealing with before you start.

title: Where AI and Blockchain Are Actually Converging (And Where They Aren't)
summary: Two of the biggest technology trends of the decade. Here's my honest read on where their combination creates genuine value and where it's just marketing.
content:
I've been asked about AI and blockchain convergence more times than I can count, usually by people who want me to validate a pitch deck.

Let me give you my actual read instead.

The genuine convergence points first.

Verifiable computation is a real problem that blockchain helps solve. If an AI model produces a result, how do you verify that result was actually produced by running that specific model with those specific inputs? Zero-knowledge proofs running on blockchain infrastructure allow you to cryptographically prove that a specific computation occurred. For AI inference that has real-world consequences — medical diagnoses, financial decisions — being able to verify the computation matters.

Data provenance for AI training is another real use case. AI models are only as reliable as their training data. Blockchain-based data marketplaces where every dataset has a verifiable chain of custody address a genuine problem in AI development.

Decentralized AI inference has valid economics in some scenarios. A marketplace where compute providers offer inference capacity and consumers pay per query, governed by smart contracts — this has viable market structure.

Now the marketing.

"AI agents managing crypto wallets" is mostly hype until we have significantly more reliable AI decision-making. The combination of autonomous financial action and currently-hallucination-prone AI should make you nervous.

"AI-generated NFTs" is an application looking for a problem. They're being combined because both words attract attention, not because the combination creates value.

The filter I apply: for any claimed convergence use case, ask whether each technology actually needs the other one. Verifiable computation needs blockchain because the whole point is trustless verification. Most other claimed combinations don't pass this test.

The technologies are both powerful. Some of their interactions will be genuinely valuable. Most of the current hype is not those interactions.

Be specific when you evaluate. It cuts through a lot of noise.

title: Why I Added Go to Our Blockchain Stack
summary: After years of Node.js backends, I added Golang to our microservices stack for specific workloads. Here's the precise reasoning.
content:
I want to be specific about this because the general argument for Go sounds like every other technology evangelism piece you've read.

We had a specific problem. A transaction processing service — taking queued transactions, submitting them to multiple chains, tracking confirmation status, retrying failures — that was handling increasing volume and showing latency spikes under load.

The Node.js implementation was not poorly written. Issues were showing up in garbage collection pauses under sustained load and in memory pressure when the queue depth spiked.

I rewrote the core processing loop in Go. Here's specifically what changed.

Go's goroutines are lighter than Node.js worker threads. Spinning up ten thousand goroutines for ten thousand pending transactions is not a problem. The concurrent processing model maps naturally to "process many transactions simultaneously while tracking each one's state independently."

The garbage collector in Go has lower worst-case latency characteristics. Under memory pressure it doesn't introduce the pauses that, in a high-frequency transaction processor, translate to visible latency spikes.

The binary deployment is a concrete operational benefit. A Go service compiles to a single static binary. Container images are smaller. Deployment is simpler.

What I didn't rewrite in Go: the API layer, the business logic, the third-party integrations. These stayed in Node.js. These services are I/O-bound, the Node.js ecosystem is mature, and there was no performance problem to solve.

The pattern I've landed on: Node.js for request-response services and API layers. Go for high-concurrency data processing and queue workers with sustained throughput requirements.

Both languages earn their place. The choice isn't philosophical — it's about matching the tool to the specific computational characteristics of the problem.

title: Decentralization Is a Spectrum, Not a Binary
summary: The Web3 community talks about decentralization as if it's a property something either has or doesn't have. It's more complicated and more interesting than that.
content:
I've noticed that "decentralized" in Web3 discourse functions as a moral category as much as a technical one.

Projects present themselves as decentralized as if this is a binary property. Critics dismiss projects as "not really decentralized" as if there's a bright line they failed to cross.

Neither framing is useful. Decentralization is a multidimensional spectrum, and understanding which dimensions matter for a given application is more valuable than debating where something falls on a single axis.

Let me break down the dimensions I think about.

Infrastructure decentralization: where are the nodes running? A blockchain with twenty validators all running on AWS in us-east-1 is technically distributed but practically centralized — a single cloud provider outage could take it down.

Governance decentralization: who can change the rules? Token-weighted governance concentrates power in proportion to wealth. On-chain governance with broad participation is more decentralized but slower and sometimes captured by large holders. Every governance model has tradeoffs.

Data decentralization: where does the data live? A DApp whose frontend is on IPFS but whose user data is in a central database is not fully decentralized. This is extremely common and often completely reasonable, but worth being clear-eyed about.

Control decentralization: who can censor transactions? A system where no party can prevent your transaction is meaningfully different from one where a central party has blocking power.

The reason this matters practically: when you're evaluating a protocol or designing a system, you need to know which dimensions of decentralization actually matter for your use case. Being vague about "decentralization" leads to systems that are decentralized where it doesn't matter and centralized where it does.

The honest answer to "is this decentralized?" is almost always "which dimension are you asking about?"

And then giving a real answer instead of a marketing one.

title: 15 Years in Enterprise IT: What I've Actually Learned
summary: A personal account of what a decade and a half of building enterprise technology actually teaches you — the lessons that don't appear in any curriculum.
content:
Fifteen years is long enough that I remember what I thought I knew when I started.

I was confident about a lot of things that turned out to be wrong. And uncertain about things that turned out to be the most important skills.

The first lesson: the technical problem is rarely the hardest problem. I've spent years on technically sophisticated systems. But the hardest parts were almost never the algorithms. They were: getting stakeholders aligned on what the system needed to do. Communicating clearly to non-technical decision-makers why a technical choice mattered. These skills weren't in my computer science education. They came from failing at them enough times.

The second lesson: systems that actually run are worth more than systems that are theoretically elegant. I've written code I'm not proud of — workarounds for legacy constraints — that has been running reliably in production for years. I've also seen beautiful, well-architected systems that never shipped because the complexity was too high. Shipping matters.

The third lesson: documentation is love. The amount of time I've spent reverse-engineering undocumented systems — written by smart people who assumed they'd always be around to explain things — is enormous. Every hour spent writing clear documentation pays back in multiples.

The fourth lesson: be suspicious of your certainties. The moments I was most confident were often the moments I was missing something.

The fifth lesson: the technology matters less than you think. I've built good systems in PHP and bad systems in Go. The quality of the system is mostly a function of the thinking, the discipline, and the communication. Tool choice explains maybe 20% of why a system is good or bad. The other 80% is the people and the process.

Fifteen more years from now I'll probably have different lessons. Some of what I believe today is wrong.

I'm fairly confident about the documentation one, though.

title: Cross-Chain Bridges: Why They Keep Getting Hacked and What To Do About It
summary: Bridge hacks have cost the industry billions. The reasons keep repeating. Here's an honest technical analysis of why, and what better designs look like.
content:
Bridge hacks have become so routine that the announcements barely make the general tech press anymore. Hundreds of millions of dollars. Then a post-mortem, a promise to do better, and three months later a different bridge gets hit.

The pattern repeats because the fundamental security challenges of bridges are hard, and the industry has been building fast without fully grappling with those challenges.

A bridge is, by definition, a system that makes commitments on two different chains simultaneously. The security of this requires that the bridge correctly observes both chains, and that the smart contracts on both sides correctly implement the logic. Each component is an attack surface.

The 2022 Ronin Bridge hack was validator compromise — the attacker compromised enough validator keys to authorize fraudulent withdrawals. The Wormhole hack was a smart contract bug — a flaw in signature verification allowed minting wrapped tokens without locking collateral. The Nomad hack was a logic error — faulty initialization meant any message could be accepted as valid.

Three different attack vectors. All resulting in massive losses.

What better designs look like:

Reduce trust to the absolute minimum. Light client bridges — where each chain maintains a light client of the other and verifies block headers directly — minimize the external validator set. Security derives from the chains' own consensus mechanisms.

Time delays on large withdrawals. A mandatory delay gives time to detect and halt an exploit before the assets leave. A UX cost worth paying.

Strict withdrawal limits. Rate limiting how much value can move through a bridge limits the blast radius of a successful exploit.

Formal verification for the critical contracts. The signature verification logic, the message format validation — these are exactly the kind of bounded systems that formal verification tools can check exhaustively.

I won't use a bridge that hasn't been extensively audited and hasn't been running in production for at least a year without major incident. That filter eliminates a lot of bridges.

I think that's the right tradeoff.
